slides.title: Security: Input Sanitization
slides.content: Input Sanitization is the critical process of 'Cleaning' and 'Validating' every piece of data that enters your system from an external source (such as a form submission). In the FRAISE `FormsManager.php`, we apply rigorous sanitization to prevent 'XSS' (Cross-Site Scripting) and 'SQL Injection' attacks that could compromise our users' security. Sanitization involves 'Stripping' dangerous HTML tags, 'Escaping' special characters, and ensuring that data matches the 'Expected Type' (e.g., ensuring a phone number only contains digits). For a professional engineer, 'Never Trust User Input' is the first commandment of security; a sanitized system is a safe system, protecting both the integrity of your database and the privacy of your users.

slides.title: Security: Authentication (AuthN)
slides.content: Authentication is the process of 'Verifying Identity'—ensuring that a user is truly who they claim to be. In the modern ERA, we utilize enterprise-grade services like **Firebase Auth** to handle this complex task, allowing users to sign in via Google with industry-standard 'Tokens' (JWT). Authentication is the 'Front Gate' of your application; without a robust identity system, you cannot protect personal data or manage user sessions. By leveraging external providers, we avoid storing sensitive passwords in our own database, significantly reducing our 'Security Liability.' Mastering authentication involves understanding 'Login Flows,' 'Session Management,' and 'Secure Token Storage,' ensuring that every access request is preceded by a verified identity.

slides.title: Security: Authorization (AuthZ / RBAC)
slides.content: Authorization is the process of 'Granting Permissions'—deciding *what* a verified user is allowed to do. In the FRAISE platform, we use **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)** to manage this, where roles like 'Admin' or 'Editor' determine access to specific CMS features and dashboard views. While *authentication* says 'Who you are,' *authorization* says 'What you can touch.' In our `php-admin-guide.md`, we define how these permissions are enforced at the router level. For a professional system, authorization must be implemented at every layer—from the 'UI Menu Items' to the 'Firestore Security Rules'—to ensure that even if a user manipulates the visual client, the underlying data remains securely protected.

slides.title: Security: Encryption At Rest
slides.content: Encryption At Rest is the protection of data that is 'Stored Locally' on a disk or database. In the FRAISE architecture, whether it's our `sites.json` list or our SQLite data files, we ensure that the physical storage is protected by OS-level file permissions and, ideally, full-disk encryption. Encryption at rest ensures that if a physical server is stolen or an unauthorized person gains access to the hard drive, the 'Raw Data' remains unreadable without the specific cryptographic key. For a professional full-stack engineer, managing 'Secrets' (like API keys in a `.env` file) and sensitive user data with 'At Rest' security is a critical part of building a foundation of trust and compliance with modern privacy regulations like GDPR.

slides.title: Security: HTTPS / TLS (Encryption in Transit)
slides.content: HTTPS (Hypertext Transfer Protocol Secure) utilizing **TLS** (Transport Layer Security) is the industry standard for 'Encryption in Transit,' ensuring that every bit of data moving between the user's browser and our server is scrambled to prevent 'Eavesdropping' or 'Man-in-the-Middle' attacks. In our FRAISE deployment, our **Nginx** configuration enforces HTTPS, and our Docker containers are isolated to prevent cross-traffic snooping. When you see the 'Green Lock' in your browser, it means that even if a hacker is listening on a public Wi-Fi network, they cannot see your 'Login Credentials' or the 'CMS Content' you are editing. Enforcing HTTPS is one of the single most important technical steps an engineer can take to protect user privacy and system integrity.

slides.title: Testing: Unit Testing
slides.content: Unit Testing is the practice of testing individual functions, methods, or components in absolute 'Isolation' to ensure they perform exactly as expected. In our modern repos (like `repo-react`), we use tools like **Vitest** or **Jest** to run these tests automatically during the development loop. A Unit Test focuses on a 'Single Path' of logic; for example, testing that a 'Slugify' function correctly converts 'Hello World' to 'hello-world.' By building a comprehensive suite of unit tests, we create a 'Safety Net' that allows us to refactor core library code with confidence, knowing that if we break a fundamental feature, the test runner will alert us instantly.

slides.title: Testing: Integration Testing
slides.content: Integration Testing shifts the focus from individual components to the 'Interaction' between different modules or services. It verifies that when two or more parts of the system are combined, they work together correctly. In the FRAISE platform, an integration test might involve checking if the `PluginManager` can successfully load and execute a hook from the `Forms` plugin, or if a Controller correctly retrieves data from a Model. Integration tests catch bugs that unit tests miss—specifically those related to 'Interface Mismatches,' 'Data Flow Errors,' and 'Timing Issues.' They are the 'Bridge' that ensures the various gears of your architectural machine are properly aligned and turning in sync.

slides.title: Testing: E2E (End-to-End)
slides.content: End-to-End (E2E) Testing simulates a 'Real User Journey' through the entire application stack, from the browser interface down to the backend database. Using professional tools like **Playwright** or **Cypress**, we record or script actions—such as 'Logging In,' 'Submitting a Form,' or 'Navigating a Dashboard'—to ensure the 'Happy Path' works for the human at the other end of the screen. E2E tests are the ultimate 'Smoke Test'; they verify that the CSS, JS, API, and DB are all functioning together in a real-world environment. While they are slower to run than unit tests, they provide the highest level of 'Confidence' that the application is actually 'Production-Ready.'

slides.title: Security: OWASP Top 10
slides.content: The **OWASP Top 10** is the globally recognized 'Security Bible' for web developers, listing the ten most critical vulnerabilities currently facing modern applications. These include threats like 'Broken Access Control,' 'Cryptographic Failures,' and 'Injection.' In the FRAISE project, we utilize the OWASP guidelines to 'Audit' our PHP classes and our AI Coder Agents, ensuring that every feature we build is resilient against common exploits. Understanding the Top 10 is not just for security specialists; it is a foundational skill for every 'Full-Stack' engineer. By 'Coding with OWASP in Mind,' you shift security to the 'Left' of the development cycle, preventing bugs before they even enter the codebase.

slides.title: The Principle of Code Reuse
slides.content: Code Reuse is the foundational engineering practice of creating 'Modular Logic' that can be utilized across multiple different parts of an application—or even multiple completely different applications. In the FRAISE project, our `repo-php/class/` directory acts as a 'Global Logic Pool,' where classes like `App.php` and `DB.php` provide services to every single site in the CMS. Effective reuse reduces 'Development Time,' minimizes 'Maintenance Effort,' and ensures that a fix in one shared module automatically propagates to the entire ecosystem. It's the hallmark of a mature architecture: instead of 'Rewriting' the same features over and over, we 'Leverage' a library of proven, tested solutions.

slides.title: DRY: Don't Repeat Yourself
slides.content: The **DRY Principle** (Don't Repeat Yourself) states that 'Every piece of knowledge must have a single, unambiguous, authoritative representation within a system.' In our FRAISE CMS, if you find yourself copying the same 'Header Logic' between `site1` and `site2`, you are violating DRY. Repeat code leads to 'Maintenance Nightmares,' where a change in a business rule requires you to update ten different files simultaneously. By applying DRY, we move that logic to a single `Component.php` class, making the system easier to understand and vastly reducing the surface area for bugs. DRY is not just about 'Lines of Code'; it's about 'Logic Ownership' and data consistency.

slides.title: Understanding Libraries
slides.content: A **Library** is a collection of pre-written, specialized code that you can 'Call' from your application to solve a specific problem—like data visualization or date formatting. In a library relationship, **You** are in control; you decide when to call the library and which parts of it you need. In our stack, we use **d3** as a library for complex data visualizations. Libraries allow you to 'Stand on the Shoulders of Giants,' leveraging the work of thousands of world-class engineers to add powerful features to your app effortlessly. Mastering library selection and management is a core skill for the modern full-stack developer, enabling you to build 'Feature-Rich' applications with a remarkably small codebase.

slides.title: Package Managers: NPM and Composer
slides.content: Package Managers are the 'Logistics Engine' of modern programming, automating the process of installing, updating, and coordinating the version dependencies of all your external libraries. For JavaScript, we use **NPM** (and its configuration file `package.json`), while in PHP, the standard is **Composer**. A package manager ensures that every developer on the team has the exact same version of every tool, preventing the 'It works on my machine' syndrome. In the FRAISE platform, our root `package.json` manages the 'Common Infrastructure' for the whole project. By mastering package management, you transition from 'Manual Configuration' to 'Automated Ecosystem Orchestration.'

slides.title: Understanding Frameworks
slides.content: A **Framework** provides the fundamental 'Skeleton' or 'Architectural Template' for your application. Unlike a library, which you call, a framework **Calls You** (a concept known as 'Inversion of Control'). It dictates where your code should live and how it should be structured. In our project, the `repo-react` and `repo-vue` folders demonstrate how different frameworks—React and Vue—own the rendering cycle and the data flow of the application. Frameworks act as 'Opininated Blueprints,' providing built-in solutions for routing, state management, and templating. They are the 'Foundation' that allows teams to build massive, scalable applications with a unified, predictable structure.

slides.title: The Role of a CMS
slides.content: A **Content Management System (CMS)** is a software application or platform that enables non-technical 'Creators' to manage digital content—like blog posts, images, and marketing pages—without writing code. In the FRAISE ecosystem, our `repo-php` is a custom CMS that stores site content in 'Structured JSON and PHP files' rather than a traditional heavy database. The CMS acts as the 'Control Interface' for the application's data. For an engineer, building or integrating a CMS is about 'Bridge Building'—creating a robust, safe environment where marketing and product teams can update a site's 'Soul' (its content) without risking its 'Body' (its technical architecture).

slides.title: Headless vs Traditional CMS
slides.content: A **Traditional CMS** (monolithic) handles everything from the 'Backend Database' to the 'Frontend Template' in one single package (like our raw PHP sites). In contrast, a **Headless CMS** removes the 'Head' (the frontend) and provides content strictly as 'Raw Data' via an API (JSON). This approach allows the content to be consumed by any device—a React web app, a mobile app, or even an IoT device. Headless is the preferred choice for modern, 'Omnichannel' digital experiences because it gives developers total freedom to use whatever frontend stack they want while maintaining a centralized, robust content management interface.

slides.title: Decoupled Architectures
slides.content: In a **Decoupled Architecture**, the 'Frontend' (presentation layer) and the 'Backend' (data layer) are independent entities that communicate over a network, typically using APIs. This is the core philosophy of our FRAISE project, which allows us to use **PHP** for core CMS logic while serving a high-performance **React** or **Vue** frontend. Decoupling provides significant advantages: you can 'Scale' the frontend independently of the backend, use different hosting providers for each, and upgrade one part of the stack without breaking the other. It is the architectural foundation of the modern 'Jamstack' movement, prioritizing 'Performance,' 'Security,' and 'Developer Experience.'

slides.title: Open Source: A Collaborative Engine
slides.content: Open Source software is based on the principle of 'Radical Transparency,' where the source code is made available for anyone to inspect, modify, and enhance. In the FRAISE project, almost every tool we use—from the **Linux** kernel to the **PHP** language and the **React** framework—is open source. Open source creates a 'Collaborative Engine' where the global developer community works together to solve common problems, resulting in software that is more 'Secure,' 'Stable,' and 'Innovative' than proprietary alternatives. For an engineer, contributing to open source is the ultimate form of 'Professional Growth,' allowing you to learn from world-class architects and build a global reputation with your code.

slides.title: Open Source Licensing
slides.content: An Open Source License is a 'Legal Document' that defines the terms under which the software can be used, modified, and redistributed. Licenses like **MIT** and **Apache** are 'Permissive,' allowing for broad use including commercial products, while the **GPL** (General Public License) is 'Copyleft,' requiring that any derivative work also be open source. Understanding licensing is critical for an engineer to ensure their work follows the law and respects the intent of the original creators. In our FRAISE project, we follow open principles to ensure that other developers can learn from, extend, and remix our architecture without legal friction. A license is a 'Foundation of Trust' in the digital commons.

slides.title: Contributing to the Ecosystem
slides.content: Contributing to the open ecosystem is not just about 'Writing Code'; it involves 'Reporting Bugs,' 'Improving Documentation,' 'Helping Others' in forums, and 'Sharing Knowledge.' In the FRAISE repo, our `/docs/` folder is a primary area for contribution, where developers can add new guides and tutorials to help the next person on the journey. Contribution is the 'Lifeblood' of high-quality software; it ensures that a project remains healthy, relevant, and well-supported. By becoming a contributor, you shift your identity from a 'Consumer of Tools' to a 'Creator of the Future,' playing an active role in shaping the technology that runs the world.

slides.title: The Proprietary vs Open Source Debate
slides.content: The choice between **Open Source** and **Proprietary** software is a strategic decision for every engineering team. Open source offers 'Transparency,' 'Customization,' and 'No Vendor Lock-in,' while proprietary software often provides 'Dedicated Support,' 'SLA Guarantees,' and 'Turn-key Solutions' for specific business problems. In the FRAISE platform, we prioritize Open Source for our core infrastructure to ensure maximum flexibility and educational value. However, we also integrate with powerful proprietary services (like high-level LLM models) when they provide a unique capability that exceeds current open alternatives. The goal is to build a 'Hybrid Strategy' that leverages the best of both worlds to achieve business and technical goals.
